content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 43 (GEN_043)

## 1. Kaved — "Heavy" vs. "Severe" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the famine was heavy in the land" | Literal k-v-d root; same root as kavod (glory/weight) |
| LOGOS | "the famine was severe in the land" | Natural English collocate for famine |
| PNEUMA | "the famine was severe in the land" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"severe" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | Both "heavy" and "severe" are valid for kaved, but "severe" is the more natural English collocate for famine. "The famine was heavy" sounds slightly unusual. The k-v-d / kavod root connection is a rich thread, but "severe" communicates the pressing, oppressive quality more effectively. |

## 2. Zimrat Ha'arets — "Choicest Produce" vs. "Finest Products" (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the choicest produce of the land" | zimrah = praised/pruned things; "produce" = agricultural |
| LOGOS | "the choicest products of the land" | "Products" is slightly broader |
| PNEUMA | "the finest products of the land" | "Finest" for the evaluative force |
| **SOPHIA** | **"choicest produce" — EMET upheld** | The items listed (balm, honey, tragacanth, laudanum, pistachios, almonds) are all agricultural/natural products. "Produce" carries a fitting agricultural resonance. "Choicest" preserves more of the Hebrew's evaluative force than "finest." |

## 3. Ka'asher Shakolti Shakalti — "As I Am Bereaved" vs. "If I Am Bereaved" (v. 14)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as I am bereaved, I am bereaved" | ka'asher = "as, just as" — resigned statement of fact |
| LOGOS | "if I am bereaved, I am bereaved" | Reads ka'asher with conditional nuance (following LXX tradition) |
| PNEUMA | "if I am to be bereaved, I will be bereaved" | Most dynamic — future conditional |
| **SOPHIA** | **"as I am bereaved, I am bereaved" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew particle ka'asher means "as, just as, according as" — it is not 'im ("if"). The LXX adds the conditional εἰ, and the traditional English rendering follows the LXX. But the Hebrew grammar supports a resigned statement of existing fact: Jacob has already been bereaved of Joseph, and he accepts whatever comes. The flat resignation is more devastating and more faithful to the Hebrew syntax. |

## 4. Rachamim — "Mercy" vs. "Compassion" (v. 14)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "may he give you mercy" | Traditional rendering of rachamim |
| LOGOS | "give you compassion" | Preserves the womb-love (rechem) connection |
| PNEUMA | "grant you mercy" | Traditional rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"compassion" — LOGOS upheld** | Rachamim appears twice in this chapter: Jacob's prayer (v. 14) and Joseph's emotional response (v. 30: nikmeru rachamav). Using "compassion" in both verses preserves the verbal thread — the father prays for compassion; the son feels it. "Mercy" is more traditional but obscures the connection. |

## 5. L'hitgolel Aleinu — "To Roll Over Us" vs. "Set Upon Us" (v. 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to roll against us and to fall upon us" | Hitpael of g-l-l = to roll oneself upon |
| LOGOS | "to roll over us and fall upon us" | Same physical image preserved |
| PNEUMA | "set upon us, overwhelm us" | Dynamic paraphrase |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to roll over us and fall upon us" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hitpael of galal is a vivid physical metaphor — the image of being rolled upon and crushed. PNEUMA's "set upon us, overwhelm us" loses the distinctive rolling image. The brothers' terror is expressed in visceral, bodily language that should be preserved. |

## 6. Nikmeru Rachamav — "Burned" vs. "Was Stirred" (v. 30)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his compassion burned" | kamar (Niphal) = to grow hot, burn with feeling |
| LOGOS | "his compassion was stirred" | Captures the involuntary upwelling |
| PNEUMA | "his compassion was stirred" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was stirred" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The Niphal of kamar means "to grow warm, be kindled, be deeply stirred." "Burned" is more intense and closer to the Hebrew heat-image, but "his compassion burned" sounds unusual in English. "Was stirred" conveys the involuntary, deep upwelling of emotion while remaining natural. The verb appears only in 1 Kgs 3:26 and Hos 11:8 — both describing overwhelming, involuntary love. |

## 7. Vayyit'appaq — "Controlled Himself" vs. "Composed Himself" (v. 31)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "controlled himself" | Hithpael of a-p-q = to restrain oneself |
| LOGOS | "controlled himself" | Same — active self-restraint |
| PNEUMA | "composed himself" | Emphasizes achieving outward calm |
| **SOPHIA** | **"controlled himself" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hithpael of aleph-p-q means "to hold oneself back, to restrain oneself." "Controlled" captures the effort of restraint. "Composed" suggests achieving calm but misses the exertion. Crucially, this verb recurs in 45:1 where Joseph "could not control himself" (lo yakol l'hit'appeq) — the release of what is here held back. Using "controlled" preserves the verbal thread between the two chapters. |

## 8. Vayyishkeru — "Were Drunk" vs. "Were Merry" (v. 34)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were drunk" | shakar = to be drunk, drink deeply |
| LOGOS | "were drunk" | Same — standard meaning of the root |
| PNEUMA | "were merry" | Softens to festive enjoyment |
| **SOPHIA** | **"were drunk" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The verb shakar is the standard Hebrew for drunkenness — the root of shikkor ("drunkard") and shekhar ("strong drink"). While it need not imply incapacitation, it clearly means more than "merry." "Were merry" sanitizes the text. The scene's emotional release — after months of terror, guilt, and famine — is expressed in uninhibited drinking. The Hebrew's plainness should be preserved. |

---

*Genesis 43: 8 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 2 items (vv. 1, 30), with EMET/LOGOS on 3 items (vv. 18, 31, 34), with EMET alone on 2 items (vv. 11, 14a — ka'asher), and with LOGOS alone on 1 item (v. 14b — rachamim/compassion). All three translators agreed on the major structural and theological decisions: Judah's surety pledge (v. 9), El Shaddai preservation (v. 14), the steward's "treasure" (v. 23), Joseph's blessing to Benjamin (v. 29), the abomination of the shared table (v. 32), the birth-order seating (v. 33), and the five-fold portion (v. 34). This was a chapter of moderate agreement — the disagreements center on register, English idiom, and one significant grammatical question (ka'asher as "as" vs. "if").*
